Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Arthur Schopenhauer

1788 – 1860

Will, representation, and the suffering subject at the root of world.

Below I use “representation” for Vorstellung even where older English translations say “idea.” I use “will” for Wille, “intellect” broadly for the knowing apparatus, and “consciousness” for Schopenhauer’s Bewusstsein and related discussions of Selbstbewusstsein where appropriate.

1. Governing thesis

A Schopenhauerian philosophy of mind is not Cartesian dualism, not materialist reductionism, and not simple panpsychism. It is best reconstructed as a transcendental-idealist, embodied, affect-centered, anti-intellectualist philosophy of mind whose deepest claim is this: consciousness is not the essence of the self; intellect is a late, bodily, brain-based function; the body is the will made visible; and the so-called soul is not an immaterial thinking substance but, at most, a mythic or traditional name for a composite phenomenon whose real root is will.

The decisive starting point is Schopenhauer’s double formula. From the standpoint of knowledge, “the world” is world-for-a-subject: objectivity always presupposes a knowing subject. From the standpoint opened by our own body, however, the world is also will: the body is not merely an object among objects, but the immediately given objectification of willing. Schopenhauer’s own text makes these two claims inseparable: the world is representation for a subject, yet the same world is also will; without the second truth, the first would leave reality spectral or merely phenomenal.

This means that Schopenhauer’s “mind” is split into layers. At the surface is representation: perception, cognition, conceptual thought, self-conscious reflection. Beneath and within it is will: impulse, striving, need, desire, affect, pain, pleasure, character, organic life. Consciousness is therefore derivative, not foundational. Will does not arise from consciousness; consciousness arises as an instrument of will. In the mature supplements, Schopenhauer says directly that intellect is subordinate and that unconscious will is the underlying reality; thought appears only late, while the will in itself is without consciousness.

That is the core of the report: Schopenhauer replaces the “soul” with will, replaces the disembodied mind with the body, replaces rational agency with motivated character, replaces the immortal ego with transindividual will, and treats theology as a symbolic discourse about the bondage and possible quieting of willing.

2. The architecture of consciousness: representation, subject, object

Schopenhauer begins from a radicalized Kantian point: the world as known is always world as representation. This is not the claim that the external world is a private fantasy. It is the claim that objectivity itself has a formal structure: every object is object-for-a-subject. Space, time, causality, empirical perception, and conceptual thought belong to the structure of representation. A world wholly outside relation to any possible subject is not something we can meaningfully know as an object.

This immediately alters philosophy of mind. Consciousness is not first a substance that then receives contents. Consciousness is a subject-object polarity. In On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, Schopenhauer says that knowing consciousness divides itself into subject and object, and that being an object for a subject is precisely being a representation. Representations then fall under structured forms of connection: causality for empirical perception, logical ground for concepts and judgments, mathematical ground for space and time, and motivation for self-conscious willing.

The subject, however, is not another object inside consciousness. Schopenhauer calls the subject that which knows but is not itself known in the same way. The body, by contrast, is an object: it appears in space and time, under causality, as one empirical item among others. This is why Schopenhauer rejects both naïve realism and crude materialism. If matter is already an object of representation, then matter cannot be the absolute explanatory ground of representation without circularity. Materialism forgets that the object “brain” itself appears only within the subject-object structure.

This does not make Schopenhauer an immaterialist in the ordinary theological or Cartesian sense. The subject is not an immortal substance; it is a formal pole of representation. The error he attacks is the assumption that one can explain consciousness simply by pointing to matter, while forgetting that “matter” is already constituted as an object of possible experience. His position is therefore a transcendental anti-reductionism, not a denial of physiology.

3. Perception is already intellectual

Schopenhauer’s theory of perception is more active than empiricism. Sensation alone does not give a world of objects. The understanding organizes sensation causally: from bodily affection it passes to an external cause, locating objects in space and time. The empirical world is thus not a raw sensory manifold but an intellectually structured representation. This is why he can say that empirical reality and transcendental ideality belong together.

Here Schopenhauer anticipates later constructivist and embodied themes in philosophy of mind. The world of experience is not inferred by explicit reasoning, but it is constituted by a pre-reflective operation of understanding. The perceiving subject does not first receive neutral sensations and then optionally interpret them; perception is already world-directed, causal, spatial, and practical.

Reason, by contrast, is secondary. It deals in abstract concepts, language, judgments, memory, planning, and reflection. It allows humans to live not only in the perceptual present but in a conceptual world of past, future, possibility, law, religion, metaphysics, and systematic deception. Yet reason is not the essence of mind. It is an instrument built upon perception, and perception itself is an instrument of will.

4. The body as the key to mind

Schopenhauer’s decisive innovation is the doctrine of the body’s double aspect. My body is given to me in two ways. Externally, it is representation: an object in space, subject to causality, visible among other bodies. Internally, it is will: not inferred, not represented from outside, but lived as striving, acting, desiring, suffering, enjoying. He says that the body is the objectivity of the will: will is the a priori knowledge of the body, and the body is the a posteriori knowledge of the will.

This yields a distinctive answer to the mind-body problem. It is not that an immaterial act of will causes a physical movement. Nor is it that a physical movement produces an illusion of willing. Rather, the act of will and the bodily act are one and the same event under two modes of access. From within, it is willing; from without, it is bodily movement. Schopenhauer explicitly says that willing and acting are not two causally connected facts but one reality seen from different sides. Bodily pleasure and pain likewise disclose the body as will: an injury is not merely a sensory datum but an immediate affection of willing life.

This is the center of Schopenhauer’s philosophy of mind. The mind is not first a thinking substance. The self is first embodied willing. Conscious thought is a later and more superficial stratum. The “I” is rooted in the lived body before it is articulated as a rational ego.

A modern label must be used carefully, but the closest approximation is a dual-aspect identity theory under transcendental idealism. The body and will are not two substances. They are one reality disclosed in two ways: representation outwardly, will inwardly. But this is not contemporary physicalist identity theory, because the physical body itself remains an appearance within representation.

5. Brain, intellect, and the circularity of physiological explanation

Schopenhauer gives physiology a major role. He repeatedly says that intellect is a function of the brain and nervous system. The brain is the organ of representation, just as the foot is the organ of walking, the hand of grasping, the stomach of digestion. A French edition of the supplements captures the point sharply: the “will to know,” viewed objectively, is the brain, just as the will to walk is the foot and the will to digest is the stomach.

But Schopenhauer also refuses to let physiology become absolute metaphysics. From the objective, scientific standpoint, representation appears as a brain process. He even says, in strikingly physiological language, that the objective world is connected with movement or affection of matter in the skull. Yet the brain itself is known only as an object in representation. Thus there is a two-way dependence: objectively, consciousness depends on the brain; transcendentally, the brain is itself an object for consciousness.

This is one of the most important tensions in Schopenhauer’s philosophy of mind. He wants both of the following claims:

First, no brain, no individual consciousness. The intellect is a brain function. The subject of knowledge is not a separable soul-substance. Human and animal consciousness are tied to nervous organization.

Second, no subject, no object called brain. The brain is not a thing-in-itself standing outside representation; it is itself an empirical object constituted under the forms of representation.

The result is not contradiction if read as a doctrine of standpoints. From within empirical science, the brain conditions consciousness. From transcendental philosophy, brain and body are already objects in the subject-object field. From metaphysics, both brain and body are objectifications of will. Schopenhauer therefore gives us neither reductive neuroscience nor disembodied idealism, but a layered account: brain as empirical condition, representation as transcendental form, will as metaphysical root.

6. Consciousness is derivative: will without knowledge

Schopenhauer is often treated as a philosopher of “will,” but the crucial point for philosophy of mind is that will is not identical with conscious volition. His will is not merely deliberate choice. It is striving, impulse, tendency, appetite, organic process, affective pressure, and the pre-cognitive directedness of life.

He explicitly says that the will is active where no knowledge guides it. Instincts, animal skills, digestion, circulation, secretion, growth, reproduction, and other vital processes are all treated as phenomena of will acting blindly through the body. In On the Will in Nature, he similarly describes unconscious will as the driving force of life and interprets organic activity as will operating without distinct representation.

This gives Schopenhauer a deep theory of the unconscious, though not yet Freud’s theory. The unconscious in Schopenhauer is not primarily a hidden theater of repressed representations. It is the pre-representational basis of organic and affective life. German and French scholarship both caution that Schopenhauer’s “unconscious” is often adjectival rather than a fully hypostatized noun: he speaks of unconscious will, unconscious organic life, and non-conscious processes, not always of “the Unconscious” as a separate psychic system. Still, modern interpreters rightly see him as a major step toward later theories of unconscious motivation.

This also means Schopenhauer is not a simple panpsychist. He universalizes will, not consciousness. The inorganic world, plants, and organic processes manifest will, but they do not thereby have conscious experience in the ordinary sense. Consciousness appears when will requires representation as an instrument: especially in animals, and in a more developed conceptual form in humans. Schopenhauer’s mature supplements state that the will in itself is without consciousness, and that consciousness arises with brain function as the will’s means of becoming aware of itself in phenomena.

A useful hierarchy can be extracted:

In inorganic nature, will appears as force, tendency, resistance, attraction, repulsion, and law-governed striving, but without consciousness.

In plants and vegetative life, will appears as growth, nutrition, reproduction, stimulus-response, and organic purposiveness, but still without representation in the animal sense.

In animals, will acquires perception, nervous organization, motives, and practical consciousness.

In human beings, will acquires reason: abstraction, language, memory, foresight, metaphysics, religion, and self-deception.

The higher levels do not abolish the lower. Human consciousness remains rooted in organic will.

7. Self-consciousness: I know myself as willing, not as pure knower

Schopenhauer’s theory of self-consciousness is one of his most important contributions. He denies that introspection gives us the subject as a knowing substance. The knower cannot become an object known in the same way as other objects. When I turn inward, what I encounter is not the pure subject of knowledge but myself as willing. In the fourth class of representations in Fourfold Root, the immediate object of inner sense is the subject of volition. The subject knows itself as willing, not as knowing.

This is why “I think” is not fundamental for Schopenhauer in the way it is for Descartes or for some readings of Kant. “I will” is more primordial than “I think.” Introspection discloses desire, aversion, effort, decision, pain, satisfaction, fear, hope, shame, anger, and striving. Schopenhauer explicitly treats emotions and feelings as states of will.

Yet this creates a problem. The knowing subject and the willing subject are somehow one. The same “I” that knows the world also finds itself willing. Schopenhauer calls this identity the “nodus” of the universe, a kind of philosophical miracle. It cannot be fully explained under the principle of sufficient reason, because that principle governs relations among representations, while the unity of knowing and willing is the point at which representation and will meet.

The resulting self is not a Cartesian ego, not a soul-substance, and not merely a bundle of representations. It is a knot: the empirical convergence of body, brain, representation, affect, character, and metaphysical will. The ego is not primary; it is a formation at the intersection of knowing and willing.

8. Motivation, character, and agency

Schopenhauer’s theory of action follows from this. Human action is not free in the ordinary libertarian sense. Motives operate as causes, but they operate in the medium of representation and self-consciousness. He defines motivation as causality seen from within. The same action can be described externally as bodily movement and internally as willing under a motive.

The will manifests through character. Given a character and a motive, action follows with necessity. We may deliberate, but deliberation itself is a process in which motives are presented, compared, intensified, weakened, and finally allowed to determine action according to the person’s character. Reason can clarify means, compare consequences, and generate abstract principles, but it does not create the fundamental direction of willing.

This is why Schopenhauer’s philosophy of mind is deeply anti-rationalist. Conscious reasons are often postures taken by deeper will. Attention, memory, conceptualization, and judgment are guided by interest. The will directs the intellect toward what matters for its aims. Schopenhauer says the intellect is naturally in servitude to the will; it is normally occupied with relations among things insofar as those relations matter for the will’s purposes.

This anticipates later theories of motivated cognition, affective bias, and rationalization. But Schopenhauer goes further: motivation is not a defect in cognition; it is the ordinary function of cognition. The intellect evolved, so to speak, to serve striving life. Disinterested knowledge is exceptional.

9. Affect, pain, pleasure, and the “heart”

Affects are not secondary decorations added to neutral cognition. For Schopenhauer, affect is the direct expression of will in consciousness. Pain is will obstructed; pleasure is will temporarily satisfied. Desire is experienced lack. Fear, hope, anger, envy, shame, and love are different modes in which will relates to its objects.

This is why Schopenhauer’s philosophy of mind is fundamentally valenced. Consciousness is not first a neutral representational screen. It is inhabited by concern. The world appears as threatening, useful, desirable, hateful, boring, sublime, or painful because it is disclosed to a willing organism. When consciousness is filled by will, Schopenhauer says, it cannot find lasting happiness or peace. Willing means restlessness; satisfaction is temporary; new lack returns.

French reception has developed this dimension with special care through the opposition of heart and head. A French study on the heart in Schopenhauer emphasizes that the heart is not merely a metaphor but a privileged physiological and symbolic expression of will, whereas the brain is the organ of representation. The “true I,” on this reading, is not in the head but in the heart: not because Schopenhauer denies the brain’s cognitive role, but because the self’s fundamental reality is willing life rather than knowledge.

This heart/head contrast is philosophically important. The head represents; the heart wills. The head can know death, ethics, religion, and metaphysics; the heart fears, desires, clings, suffers, and resists. Theology often speaks in the language of the heart because it addresses the will more than the intellect.

10. Aesthetic consciousness: the temporary suspension of will

Schopenhauer’s aesthetics is also a theory of consciousness. In ordinary cognition, the intellect serves the will. In aesthetic contemplation, however, consciousness is temporarily freed from practical interest. The individual forgets personal willing and becomes, in Schopenhauer’s famous phrase, a pure subject of knowledge. The object is no longer perceived merely in relation to use, danger, desire, or possession; it is contemplated as Idea.

This is not entertainment theory. It is a theory of altered consciousness. The self as needy individual recedes. Attention becomes object-centered rather than ego-centered. The pain of willing is suspended because the will, for a time, no longer dominates consciousness. In the supplements, Schopenhauer describes the intellect’s emancipation from the will as rare and temporary: the intellect, normally a servant, can momentarily move freely, no longer belonging to a particular will.

The aesthetic state therefore reveals two things. First, consciousness is usually will-bound. Second, consciousness can sometimes become will-less, or at least less governed by personal striving. This gives Schopenhauer a proto-phenomenology of contemplative experience: absorption, de-individualization, non-instrumental attention, and relief from self-concern.

Spanish-language scholarship on Schopenhauer’s “better consciousness” is helpful here. It stresses that the issue is not merely an epistemic division between empirical and higher knowledge, but a transformation in how consciousness conducts itself in a suffering subject. In art, and more radically in saintly or ascetic practice, consciousness becomes capable of suspending or overcoming its ordinary bondage to willing.

11. Compassion and other minds

Schopenhauer’s ethics belongs inside his philosophy of mind because it concerns how the self relates to other centers of suffering. Egoism arises from the principium individuationis: I experience myself as this individual, separated from other individuals by space, time, body, and interest. Moral insight occurs when this separation is partially penetrated. The suffering of another is no longer merely an external fact; it is felt as significant in a way analogous to my own suffering.

Schopenhauer explicitly connects virtue with the Vedic formula “Tat tvam asi” — “this thou art.” The good person recognizes his own inner being in other living beings, and this recognition grounds compassion, love, and self-sacrifice. He also states the ethical thesis with unusual directness: all pure love is sympathy, and genuine goodness arises from the penetration of the principle of individuation.

This is not a standard inference-to-other-minds theory. Schopenhauer does use analogy from my body to other bodies, but his deeper account is metaphysical: the same will objectifies itself in all beings. Compassion is therefore not merely a cognitive conclusion; it is a mode of affective-metaphysical recognition. Other minds are not inferred as alien interiors hidden behind bodies; they are disclosed through shared will, shared suffering, and the collapse of egoic separateness.

This is also why animals matter in Schopenhauer’s ethics. Since animals are objectifications of will and possess suffering consciousness, they are not morally negligible. A philosophy of mind that centers will and suffering cannot restrict moral relevance to rational concept-use.

12. Soul, death, and immortality

Schopenhauer’s critique of the soul is central. He rejects the traditional doctrine of a simple, immaterial, immortal thinking substance. In the supplements, he notes that philosophers often assume a soul as simple, immaterial, and immortal, but he does not accept that these attributes follow. The subject of knowledge is not a substance; it is tied to brain activity and to the phenomenal organism.

Individual consciousness, for Schopenhauer, does not survive death. What death destroys is the knowing consciousness bound to the brain and individual organism. What is indestructible is not the personal ego, memory, or rational soul, but will as the inner reality of the phenomenon. Schopenhauer is careful: indestructibility is not personal continuance. The true being of the individual is not annihilated, but the individual as conscious person does not continue in the ordinary theological sense.

This is a severe revision of immortality. He wants to preserve something metaphysically deeper than material annihilation, while denying personal survival. Death ends the empirical subject, but not the will-in-itself that appeared as that subject. The individual is a wave; the will is the sea. But the wave does not survive as that wave.

This also explains his interest in metempsychosis and palingenesis. He does not endorse a crude personal reincarnation in which “I,” with my memories and ego, simply migrate intact. Rather, he finds in Indian and Buddhist traditions a mythic approximation to his view that the individual form perishes while will continues beyond the individual. He treats doctrines of transmigration as religious symbols closer to his metaphysical truth than doctrines of a created soul with endless personal post-mortem existence.

The theological implication is sharp: the “soul,” if the word is retained, must not mean an immortal thinking ego. It means either the will as the true kernel of the person, or the mythic representation of something deeper than empirical consciousness. In Schopenhauer’s system, the soul is dethroned by will.

13. Theology is not a distraction

Schopenhauer’s theological comments are not ornamental. They are part of his philosophy of mind because religion, for him, expresses in mythic form the structure, bondage, guilt, suffering, and possible redemption of willing consciousness.

He is not a theist in the ordinary sense. The world-root is not a rational creator, providential mind, or benevolent divine person. In fact, he attacks physico-theology precisely because it projects intellect and design onto the origin of things, whereas for him intellect is late and secondary. The underlying reality is unconscious will, not divine reason.

Yet Schopenhauer does not dismiss religion as merely stupid. In his dialogue on religion, he treats religion as a symbolic or allegorical vehicle that can preserve moral-metaphysical truths for people who do not live by philosophical abstraction. Religions may be theoretically false in literal form while morally or practically true in their deeper tendency.

This is why Christian theology matters to him. He interprets original sin as the affirmation of the will to life itself. Salvation is the denial or quieting of that will. Christ becomes, in his reading, the symbol or personification of the denial of will, not primarily a dogmatic object of supernatural belief. He is especially drawn to Augustinian and Lutheran anti-Pelagian themes because they imply that the will is not simply free to redeem itself by rational decision.

This has direct philosophy-of-mind significance. “Original sin” names the fact that ordinary consciousness is structurally bound to egoic willing. “Grace” names the fact that genuine transformation of the will cannot be manufactured by deliberate egoic willing, because the ego is itself an expression of the problem. “Salvation” names a radical transformation in the relation between consciousness and will: from affirmation, desire, and clinging toward resignation, compassion, asceticism, and will-lessness.

Schopenhauer also gives serious attention to Brahmanism, Buddhism, and the Upanishads. He sees in them doctrines of illusion, individuation, suffering, asceticism, compassion, and identity beyond the ego. His citation of the Upanishadic idea that all creatures are, in a deeper sense, oneself supports his moral and metaphysical account of compassion.

His comparative theology is selective and often polemical. He tends to privilege Indian traditions and an ascetic interpretation of Christianity while criticizing religious optimism, creationism, and world-affirming theologies. He also makes historically and theologically questionable contrasts among religions, especially when ranking Christianity, Buddhism, Brahmanism, Judaism, and Islam. Those judgments should not be repeated uncritically. But the philosophical point remains: for Schopenhauer, the deepest religious doctrines are symbolic accounts of the will’s bondage and possible negation.

14. Christianity, Buddhism, pessimism, and consciousness

Schopenhauer’s preference for pessimistic and ascetic religion is not incidental. A religion is profound for him when it recognizes that ordinary existence is suffering because willing is suffering. Desire produces lack; satisfaction is temporary; individuality produces egoism; the world is not a harmonious rational order designed for happiness.

In this sense, he sees an affinity between what he takes to be the inner spirit of Christianity and Indian religious thought. The truth of Christianity, for him, is not ecclesiastical power or dogmatic literalism, but resignation, poverty, chastity, compassion, suffering, and the denial of the will. The same tendency appears, in his view, in Buddhist asceticism and the renunciatory traditions of India.

The philosophy-of-mind content is that salvation is not primarily belief, nor intellectual assent, nor moral rule-following. It is a transformation of desire. Religion addresses the will more than the intellect. Dogma is secondary; conversion of consciousness and willing is primary.

This also explains why he can treat the devil as philosophically useful. In the dialogue on religion, Schopenhauer’s speaker suggests that the devil functions as a counterweight to the doctrine of an all-good God, because otherwise the evil and suffering of the world become unintelligible under theism. Again, the theological symbol is important because it encodes a metaphysical psychology: the world is not governed by benevolent rationality; it is driven by blind striving, conflict, and suffering.

15. The “better consciousness” and the path beyond ordinary mind

A comprehensive Schopenhauerian philosophy of mind must include not only ordinary consciousness but transformed consciousness. Ordinary mind is will-bound. Aesthetic mind is temporarily will-less. Moral mind penetrates individuation through compassion. Ascetic mind turns against the will to life itself.

Spanish scholarship on Schopenhauer’s besseres Bewusstsein, or “better consciousness,” is especially useful here. It emphasizes that this is not merely a second cognitive faculty that gives theoretical knowledge of the thing-in-itself. It is a mode of consciousness that emerges when the suffering subject begins to overcome ordinary empirical willing. The philosopher may present this theoretically, the artist may disclose it aesthetically, and the saint may embody it practically.

This helps correct an overly Anglophone analytic reading that reduces Schopenhauer’s mind-theory to epistemology plus metaphysics. The system is also soteriological. Consciousness is not merely something to be explained; it is something to be transformed. The highest question is not “How does the brain produce representations?” but “Can consciousness cease to serve the will that makes existence suffering?”

16. Schopenhauer and the unconscious: proto-Freudian, but not Freudian

Schopenhauer is often called a precursor of Freud, and with good reason: he places unconscious striving beneath conscious rationality; he treats sexuality, desire, repression-like self-deception, dream, affect, and irrational motivation as central; and he denies that the conscious ego rules the psyche.

But the comparison must be disciplined. Schopenhauer’s unconscious is broader and more metaphysical than Freud’s. It includes the whole pre-conscious depth of organic and natural striving, not only repressed wishes or infantile sexuality. German interpretation has noted that Schopenhauer’s will may be read in modern terms as unconscious motivational processes, but this remains an interpretation: Schopenhauer’s will is not simply the brain’s unconscious processing. It is the metaphysical inner being of the organism and, by extension, of nature.

There is also a methodological difference. Freud remains, at least aspirationally, a clinical theorist of psychic mechanisms. Schopenhauer is a metaphysician. He uses introspection of willing, embodiment, suffering, and organic life to generalize beyond psychology into ontology. That generalization is philosophically powerful but vulnerable: it risks projecting human affective structure onto all of nature.

17. Animal magnetism and the limits of Schopenhauer’s empirical reach

Schopenhauer’s interest in animal magnetism, magic, and related phenomena is a useful warning. He sometimes treats alleged magnetic effects as evidence that will can act beyond ordinary conscious mediation. In On the Will in Nature, he discusses magnetism in terms of will, belief, and the organism as objectivity of will.

This material should not be accepted as sound empirical science. Its importance is diagnostic. It shows how far Schopenhauer was willing to extend the primacy of will beyond ordinary cognition and voluntary action. Philosophically, the lesson is that he wanted a theory of agency and embodiment deeper than conscious intention. Historically, however, these passages also show the danger of using metaphysics to overinterpret dubious phenomena.

18. Extracted Schopenhauerian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive reconstruction can be stated as a set of theses.

First, the correlation thesis: every object of knowledge is a representation for a subject. Mind is not one object among others; the subject-object structure is the form of all objectivity.

Second, the embodiment thesis: the body is the privileged object because it is known both externally as representation and internally as will. This makes bodily self-awareness more fundamental than detached cognition.

Third, the primacy of will thesis: willing, striving, affect, impulse, and organic life are more fundamental than intellect. Consciousness is not the source of will; it is an instrument developed by will.

Fourth, the brain-function thesis: intellect and individual consciousness depend empirically on the brain and nervous system. There is no disembodied personal intellect and no traditional immortal rational soul.

Fifth, the anti-reduction thesis: although consciousness depends empirically on the brain, the brain itself is an object within representation. Therefore physiology is indispensable but not metaphysically ultimate.

Sixth, the affective thesis: feelings are states of will. Pleasure, pain, desire, fear, boredom, and satisfaction are not secondary mental ornaments but basic disclosures of embodied willing.

Seventh, the motivation thesis: action is causality from within. Motives determine action through character. Rational deliberation is real but subordinate to the structure of willing.

Eighth, the unconscious thesis: most willing is unconscious. Organic functions, instinct, bodily life, and much motivation occur beneath explicit self-consciousness.

Ninth, the aesthetic-transformation thesis: consciousness can temporarily become will-less in aesthetic contemplation, suspending egoic desire and suffering.

Tenth, the moral-transformation thesis: compassion arises when consciousness penetrates individuation and recognizes the same will in others.

Eleventh, the ascetic-soteriological thesis: the deepest transformation is not better reasoning but the quieting or denial of the will to life.

Twelfth, the anti-personal-immortality thesis: individual consciousness perishes with the organism, but the will as inner reality is not annihilated. This is not personal immortality.

Thirteenth, the theological-symbolic thesis: religious doctrines are myths or allegories that may express truths about will, suffering, guilt, compassion, death, and salvation more powerfully than literal metaphysics.

19. Relation to present philosophy of mind

Schopenhauer reframes the mind-body problem. He does not ask, in a modern physicalist way, how matter produces consciousness as an emergent property. Nor does he ask, in a Cartesian way, how a mental substance interacts with a physical body. He asks how the same body can be given outwardly as representation and inwardly as will. The body is the bridge.

On consciousness, he is neither a standard representationalist nor a standard qualia theorist. Consciousness is object-directed representation, but affective self-disclosure is equally basic. Pain is not merely a quale attached to a physical process; it is the will’s obstruction felt from within. Pleasure is not a positive metaphysical good but the temporary removal of lack.

On intentionality, Schopenhauer’s position is will-based. Cognition is normally interested cognition. Objects appear as motives, threats, tools, obstacles, and satisfactions. Pure theoretical objectivity is exceptional and fragile.

On the self, he is a powerful anti-substantialist. The self is not an immortal ego. It is the empirical unity of organism, brain, representation, character, and will. The pure subject of knowledge is formal and objectless; the empirical self is willing body.

On agency, he is a compatibilist only in a strained sense. Empirical freedom is denied: actions follow from motives and character. But metaphysical freedom belongs to will as thing-in-itself, outside the principle of sufficient reason. Human beings are responsible not because they could arbitrarily have chosen otherwise at a moment, but because action expresses what they are.

On the unconscious, he is one of the most important pre-Freudian philosophers. But his unconscious is ontological and organic before it is clinical.

On religion and mind, he is unusually deep: theology is a symbolic psychology of will, suffering, and redemption.

20. Principal tensions and unresolved problems

The first major problem is epistemological. If all knowledge is representation, how can Schopenhauer know that the thing-in-itself is will? He answers that our own body gives us a unique inner access: we do not merely observe it; we live it as willing. But he also admits that even this knowledge is not exhaustive knowledge of the thing-in-itself, because it still appears under temporal and experiential forms. This is the central tension of the system.

The second problem is the analogy from self to world. From my body I know will inwardly. But why conclude that all nature is will? Schopenhauer argues that this is the best metaphysical interpretation of the world’s striving, force, conflict, growth, and organic purposiveness. But critics can object that he projects a human category onto non-human reality.

The third problem is the brain-consciousness circle. Empirically, the brain conditions consciousness. Transcendentally, the brain is an object in consciousness. Schopenhauer’s standpoint method makes this intelligible, but it does not remove all discomfort. It is a deliberately non-reductive circularity.

The fourth problem is ambiguity in “will.” Sometimes it means conscious volition. Sometimes it means affective desire. Sometimes organic impulse. Sometimes metaphysical reality. Sometimes natural force. Schopenhauer knows this expansion is bold, but the breadth of the term can make the doctrine look both profound and unstable.

The fifth problem is theology. Schopenhauer’s religious interpretations are philosophically rich but historically selective. His Christianity is heavily ascetic, Augustinian, and anti-optimistic; his Buddhism and Brahmanism are filtered through nineteenth-century European sources; his polemics against some religious traditions often oversimplify them. These limitations matter. Still, his theological readings are not digressions; they are central to his account of mind as will seeking, suffering, and possible release.

21. Final synthesis

Schopenhauer’s philosophy of mind can be compressed into one formula:

The conscious mind is the brain’s representational instrument of an embodied will; the self is not a thinking soul but a lived body whose inner reality is striving; cognition normally serves desire, but in art, compassion, and ascetic resignation consciousness can partially or radically free itself from the will it ordinarily serves.

This is why his theory remains philosophically fertile. It gives us an embodied theory of selfhood, a non-Cartesian theory of mind-body identity, a critique of rational agency, a theory of unconscious motivation, an affective theory of consciousness, a transformation-oriented account of aesthetic and religious experience, and a severe critique of personal immortality. It also gives us a warning: any philosophy of mind that begins with cognition, computation, or representation alone has already started too late. For Schopenhauer, beneath the mind that knows is the body that suffers, desires, fears, and strives; beneath the body as object is will; and beyond ordinary willing lies the religious and ethical question of whether the will can be quieted at all.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
